text: This video demonstrates one of the approaches to laparoscopic suturing and knot tying. The technique demonstrated here is the technique developed by Dr. James Butch Rosser. Others, including Dr. Zoltan Szabo, have developed uniquely different approaches in which the needle itself is not grasped in the process of knot tying. We will begin with a review of the techniques of the Rosser approach to laparoscopic suturing.

The surgeon introduces the needle into the abdomen through a ten millimeter port using the non-dominant hand, grasping the suture three centimeters behind the swedge. The dominant hand then grasps the needle one-third of the distance from the swedge to the tip for tissue penetration. The needle enters the tissue at a sixty to seventy-five degree angle and is rotated through the tissue following its curve, with the non-dominant hand stabilizing the tissue. The tip of the needle is grasped as it emerges through the tissue, and the grasper is locked to secure the needle.

The surgeon completes the laparoscopic suturing by tying the knot. The needle is held at a 45-degree angle in the non-dominant hand, positioned north of the knot. The first wrap involves two clockwise turns with the needle to the left of the knot and the suture tag on the right. The dominant hand instrument falls on top of the tag after rotation. The non-dominant hand moves to the one o'clock position, while the dominant hand leaves the tag at seven o'clock. The second wrap involves a counterclockwise rotation, with the non-dominant hand at eleven o'clock and the dominant hand at five o'clock. The final wrap is a single clockwise turn, completing the surgeon's knot.
